skill.name: neon-postgres
skill.description: Guidance for building with Neon Serverless Postgres, including setup, connection methods, branching, authentication, APIs, SDKs, and CLI workflows.
skill.summary: Use official Neon guidance to design, connect, and operate Serverless Postgres projects.
skill.author: neondatabase

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown backticks, Neon documentation URLs, and text that resembled weak crypto or reconnaissance patterns. Review found no executable code, no prompt injection attempt, and no evidence of malicious intent. The remaining risk is expected network documentation access and optional Neon CLI guidance.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static execution findings point to Markdown formatting, package names, and documentation references. The reviewed file contains no script body, dynamic execution, or hidden shell payload.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown guidance and inline command examples. There is no executable source file or user input interpolation at those locations.

low_findings.title: Expected Links to Official Neon Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs all reference neon.com documentation pages. Network access is part of the skill purpose, but users should understand that responses may fetch external documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed links consistently target official Neon documentation. I found no non-Neon endpoint, webhook, paste site, or credential exfiltration pattern.

low_findings.title: High-Severity Static Blockers Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance matches are prose, documentation paths, or a hostname suffix example. No cryptographic operation or host enumeration command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines describe docs, an index, connection pooling, and logical replication. They do not contain code that hashes, encrypts, scans hosts, or gathers system details.

content.value_statement: Neon setup and operations can be hard to align with each runtime. This skill points agents to official Neon guidance for practical database decisions.
actual_capabilities: Explains Neon architecture, projects, branches, endpoints, autoscaling, and scale-to-zero behavior.
Guides first-time setup, driver selection, and connection string planning.
Compares TCP, HTTP, WebSocket, edge, serverless, and long-running connection patterns.
Points to Neon Auth, Neon JS SDK, and data API documentation for application integration.
Supports CLI, MCP, REST API, TypeScript SDK, and Python SDK planning.
Covers branching, restore, read replicas, pooling, allow lists, and logical replication topics.
limitations: It depends on current Neon documentation for final accuracy.
It does not create or modify Neon resources without separate tool access.
It cannot replace database security review for production systems.
It may require user-provided project details for precise recommendations.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Neon Project
use_cases.description: Choose setup steps, connection methods, and initial database structure for a new application.

use_cases.title: Design Preview Database Workflows
use_cases.description: Use Neon branching for isolated environments, schema migration checks, and deployment previews.

use_cases.title: Automate Neon Operations
use_cases.description: Map CLI, REST API, and SDK options for repeatable project, branch, and endpoint management.

prompt_templates.title: Explain Neon Basics
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain how Neon Serverless Postgres works for a developer who already knows basic Postgres.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Connection Method
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose the best Neon connection method for a serverless application with bursty traffic.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Branching for Previews
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Neon branching workflow for pull request previews, schema migrations, and cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Map API Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Neon CLI, REST API, TypeScript SDK, and Python SDK options for automated environment provisioning.

output_examples.input: I need a Neon setup plan for a Next.js application.
output_examples.output: A staged plan covering project creation, driver selection, connection pooling, environment variables, local development, and production checks.

output_examples.input: We want database branches for pull request previews.
output: Recommended branch lifecycle for preview environments.
Suggested cleanup rules and naming conventions.
Notes about schema migration testing and restore options.

output_examples.input: Our serverless API opens too many Postgres connections.
output_examples.output: A concise diagnosis that compares pooling, driver choices, endpoint hostnames, and runtime-specific connection behavior.

best_practices: Verify detailed answers against current Neon documentation before implementing production changes.
Share runtime, framework, traffic, and security constraints when asking for connection advice.
Use least-privilege API keys and separate credentials for development, preview, and production.
anti_patterns: Do not assume one connection method fits every runtime.
Do not expose Neon connection strings, passwords, or API keys in prompts.
Do not run CLI or API changes against production without review and rollback planning.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents answer Neon Serverless Postgres questions using official topic guidance and documentation links.

faq.question: Can it create Neon projects?
faq.answer: It can guide creation workflows, but actual changes require separate CLI, API, MCP, or user-approved tool access.

faq.question: Does it support application connection choices?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers TCP, HTTP, WebSocket, edge, serverless, and long-running connection patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with Neon Auth?
faq.answer: Yes. It points agents to Neon Auth and Neon JS SDK guidance for application authentication workflows.

faq.question: Is this only for JavaScript developers?
faq.answer: No. It includes general Postgres guidance plus Neon CLI, REST API, TypeScript SDK, and Python SDK topics.

faq.question: What should users avoid sharing?
faq.answer: Users should avoid sharing database passwords, connection strings, API keys, and production secrets in prompts.
